name: Fivetran ODI Demo Scoring Rubric
description: Scores a Fivetran demo video against ODI messaging standards on a 100-point scale.

categories.name: Business Problem Opening
categories.description: Does the demo open with the customer's business problem before touching any product? A great demo makes the buyer feel seen before a single feature is shown.
categories.great: Opens with a specific, quantified customer pain. No product mentioned in the first 60 seconds. Buyer says 'that's exactly my problem.'
categories.good: Opens with a general problem statement before the demo. Some product framing too early.
categories.poor: Opens immediately with product UI or feature walkthrough. Business context is absent or tacked on at the end.

categories.name: ODI Story Completeness
categories.description: Does the demo tell the full Fivetran ODI story: source → Fivetran ingest → open format (Iceberg/Delta) → dbt transform → destination/analytics? Partial coverage scores proportionally.
categories.great: Covers the full arc end-to-end. Fivetran, dbt, and destination are all present and connected as a unified story.
categories.good: Covers 2 of 3 layers. The connection between layers is explained but not fully shown.
categories.poor: Single-layer demo (connector only, or destination only). No mention of the broader pipeline.

categories.name: Technical Accuracy
categories.description: Are all technical claims, product names, feature descriptions, and behaviors accurate? Errors here destroy credibility with technical buyers.
categories.great: Zero factual errors. Correct terminology throughout (BLR not LogMiner, MDLS not S3, etc.). Nuanced claims are appropriately qualified.
categories.good: Minor inaccuracies that do not mislead. Product names and capabilities are broadly correct.
categories.poor: Material errors in feature descriptions, product names, or technical claims that would confuse or mislead a technical buyer.

categories.name: Demo Execution Quality
categories.description: Does the demo run smoothly? Dead ends, errors, confusion, and backtracking all signal unpreparedness and erode buyer confidence.
categories.great: Smooth end-to-end. Any errors are handled gracefully. No dead ends. The demo tells the story it set out to tell.
categories.good: Minor stumbles but recovers cleanly. The core demo points land.
categories.poor: Multiple errors, dead ends, or confusion visible on screen. Sync fails, UI breaks, or demo has to be abandoned mid-flow.

categories.name: Audience Targeting
categories.description: Is the language, depth, and framing calibrated to the right buyer (CDO/CIO vs data engineer vs DBA)? A great demo speaks the audience's language.
categories.great: Vocabulary, depth, and examples are perfectly matched to the stated or implied audience. Would not confuse a CDO or bore a DBA.
categories.good: Mostly calibrated but drifts too technical or too high-level at points.
categories.poor: Clearly wrong audience. DBA-level detail for a CDO, or business fluff for a technical engineer.

categories.name: Objection Handling & Differentiation
categories.description: Does the demo proactively address common objections (data residency, security, maintenance burden, existing tools) and differentiate from alternatives?
categories.great: Top 2-3 objections addressed before the buyer raises them. Clear, factual differentiation from alternatives (not feature-bashing).
categories.good: One or two objections addressed. Differentiation is present but reactive.
categories.poor: No objection handling. Differentiation absent or vague ('we're the best at X').

categories.name: Narrative Arc & Flow
categories.description: Does the demo have a clear beginning (problem), middle (solution in action), and end (outcome/proof)? Is it organized or does it wander?
categories.great: Clean three-act structure. Each transition is deliberate. Buyer always knows where they are in the story.
categories.good: Generally organized but transitions are abrupt or some sections feel disconnected.
categories.poor: No clear arc. Wanders between features without a through-line. Buyer loses context.

categories.name: Closing & Call to Action
categories.description: Does the demo close with a clear summary of what was shown and a concrete next step for the buyer?
categories.great: Crisp recap of what was demonstrated. Explicit next step (POC offer, follow-up, documentation link).
categories.good: Summary present but next step is vague.
categories.poor: Demo ends abruptly or just trails off. No recap, no next step.

core_pillars: One copy of data in open formats (Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake) — no proprietary lock-in
Fivetran as the ingest layer: 700+ certified connectors, CDC via Binary Log Reader, schema auto-registration
dbt as the transform layer: bronze/silver/gold medallion, tested, documented gold models
Any query engine reads the same bytes: Snowflake, Databricks, Athena, Trino — pluggable
Hybrid deployment answers the data-residency objection without changing the architecture
AI-ready: the same governed gold layer feeds both BI and AI agents
common_objections_to_address: We already have Snowflake/Databricks — why do we need Fivetran?
Our data can't leave our network
We have homegrown pipelines that already work
We use Autoloader / Lakeflow / native connectors already
dbt is open source, why pay for Fivetran Transformations?
odi_messaging_context.what_great_looks_like: A great ODI demo makes the buyer feel the pain of fragmented data infrastructure, then shows Fivetran as the unified foundation. It proves the full pipeline works — data lands, transforms, and surfaces in analytics or AI — and leaves the buyer with a clear mental model of how their existing environment maps to ODI.
